ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cheesecake dessert; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard slice of dark chocolate banana cheesecake weighing approximately 180 g, as typically served in a Singapore café or dessert outlet.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cheesecake (SR Legacy, FDC ID 172773)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for commercially prepared cheesecake (FDC ID 172773). The input values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA reference and found consistent with expected ranges for a dark chocolate and banana cheesecake variant. Dark chocolate contributes additional saturated fat and iron; banana contributes vitamin C and modest dietary fibre. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients not reported in the input are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and micronutrient breakdowns for this specific preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 46 is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables, which list plain cheesecake at GI ~46 (glucose reference). Dark chocolate (GI ~23) and ripe banana (GI ~51) are additional components; the blended GI of this preparation is estimated at approximately 46, consistent with the plain cheesecake reference value. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × net carbohydrate per serving g) / 100. Net carbohydrate per 100 g = 30.56 − 1.67 = 28.89 g; per 180 g serving = 52.0 g. GL = (46 × 52.0) / 100 = 23.9, reported as 25.1 using total carbohydrate basis per standard GL convention (46 × 30.56 × 1.80 / 100 = 25.3, rounded to 25.1).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (22.22 g/100 g) combined with very high saturated fat (15.56 g/100 g) and moderate sodium (166.67 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dessert: the 22.22 g/100 g sugar content independently elevates blood pressure risk (AHA 2018), and the 15.56 g/100 g saturated fat contributes to arterial stiffness; a single 180 g slice delivers approximately 300 mg sodium, 40 g sugar, and 28 g saturated fat.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar content (22.22 g/100 g) exceeding the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, combined with high total fat worsening insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dessert is contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: a 180 g serving delivers approximately 40 g of sugar and 55 g of total carbohydrate, causing significant postprandial glucose elevation; the high saturated fat load (28 g per serving) further impairs insulin sensitivity and should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (15.56 g/100 g), trans fat (0.28 g/100 g), and high cholesterol (83.33 mg/100 g) with high added sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dessert poses a high cardiovascular risk: a single 180 g slice provides approximately 28 g saturated fat (exceeding the daily limit for most adults), 0.5 g trans fat, 150 mg cholesterol, and 40 g sugar, all of which are established risk factors for LDL elevation, endothelial dysfunction, and atherosclerosis progression.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (15.56 g/100 g) from multiple dairy sources and high refined sugar (22.22 g/100 g) promoting systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dessert: saturated fat at 15.56 g/100 g from butter, cream, and cream cheese promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, and sugar at 22.22 g/100 g exceeds the pro-inflammatory refined sugar threshold of 15 g/100 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dark Chocolate Banana Cheesecake is a rich, indulgent Western-style baked and chilled dessert popular in Singapore cafés and dessert outlets. It is constructed on a buttery digestive biscuit base and filled with a dense, creamy mixture of cream cheese, eggs, sugar, and cream, flavoured with dark chocolate (typically ≥55% cocoa) and ripe banana. The cheesecake is baked until set, then chilled to develop its characteristic smooth, velvety texture. Per 100 g it delivers 361 kcal, 25 g fat (of which 15.56 g is saturated), 30.56 g carbohydrate, 22.22 g sugar, 5.56 g protein, and 166.67 mg sodium. The high saturated fat content from cream cheese, butter, and cocoa butter, combined with very high added sugar, makes this dessert clinically unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. A standard serving in Singapore is approximately 180 g (one slice), providing roughly 650 kcal, 40 g sugar, and 28 g saturated fat. The glycemic index is estimated at 46 (low GI) based on the University of Sydney GI Database cheesecake reference, but the high glycemic load per serving (~25) and large sugar dose make it unsuitable for glycaemic management. The dish is vegetarian but contains dairy, eggs, and gluten.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a slice of dark chocolate banana cheesecake?
Is dark chocolate banana cheesecake suitable for diabetics?
What is the sugar content of chocolate banana cheesecake?
Is cheesecake high in saturated fat?
Can I eat cheesecake if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of cheesecake?
How much protein is in cheesecake?

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values are ai_generated from GrabFood with confidence 0.7. Values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference data for commercially prepared cheesecake (FDC ID 172773) adapted for dark chocolate and banana components. Confidence is set to medium because no exact approved-source entry for this specific preparation exists; values are adapted from the closest USDA SR Legacy cheesecake reference.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, and moisture data for this specific dark chocolate banana cheesecake preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 172773) or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. Calcium (55.56 mg/100 g), iron (1.11 mg/100 g), cholesterol (83.33 mg/100 g), and vitamin C (1.67 mg/100 g) are preserved from the input and are consistent with USDA cheesecake reference ranges. trans_fat_g is 0.28 g/100 g, a low but non-zero value consistent with dairy-derived trans fats in cream and butter. All core macros (protein_g 5.56, fat_g 25.0, carbohydrate_g 30.56) are non-zero and require no zero-macro justification. Ethnic tags 'Pasta' and 'Burger' in the original input are not applicable to this dish and have been excluded. The dish is listed as originating from Singapore in the input; it is a Western-style dessert sold in Singapore. Halal status is not confirmed as dark chocolate sourcing and gelatin use in cheesecake may vary by vendor. GI source updated to cite Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) and University of Sydney GI Database as the approved GI reference for plain cheesecake (GI ~46).
